rag_rules: Fix unresolved references by using only visible identifiers from the current component scope; do not introduce new architecture.
When AGREE reports type mismatches, ensure expressions match the expected type (int, real, bool) and avoid <error> types.
Do not place implementation-level guarantees in component type scopes; move valid bindings to implementation annexes.
Delete invalid AGREE clauses that reference undefined or inaccessible identifiers rather than attempting to define them.
generic_rules: AGREE expressions must preserve type consistency and use only declared or visible identifiers.
Component type contracts should reference only features available at that scope; implementation bindings go in implementation annexes.
Validator diagnostics pointing to unresolved identifiers should be resolved by removing or rewriting clauses, not by adding new architecture.
Avoid introducing package-qualified names or types not present in the input AADL model.
syntax_or_pattern_reminders: AGREE contracts use `assume`, `guarantee`, `eq`, `const`, and `assign` with proper type annotations.
Assignments in implementation scope must target valid output features or declared auxiliary variables.
Helper functions and variables must be declared within the same annex or imported from valid packages.
Type consistency is required: numeric expressions for int/real, Boolean for bool.
anti_patterns: Do not attempt to define new identifiers or types not present in the AADL model when resolving validator errors.
Do not place implementation-level guarantees in component type annexes; this causes scope resolution errors.
Do not assume that AADL property names (e.g., Period, Deadline) are automatically available as AGREE variables.
Do not generate AGREE code that introduces package-qualified references without matching `with` clauses.
irrelevant_topics: AADL package references and `with` clause requirements
AGREE annex placement in component types vs. implementations
Model fusion and owner/scope preservation rules
